citation: de Groat, G. (2015). A history of video game cataloging in U.S. libraries. Cataloging & Classification Quarterly, 53(2), 135-156. https://doi.org/10.1080/01639374.2014.954297
summary:
de Groat provides a technical history of video game cataloging in U.S. libraries, tracing how descriptive rules, MARC coding, subject/genre practice, and title authority work struggled to accommodate games from the late 1970s through the transition from AACR2 to RDA. The article is not about public-facing gaming programs, but it is central to GBLS because collections, advisory, access, preservation, and scholarship all depend on whether games can be described, collocated, searched, limited, and distinguished in library catalogs.

The article begins from a blunt premise: video games have always been an awkward fit in regular cataloging workflows. There have never been specific descriptive cataloging rules for video games, so they were usually treated under broader and constantly changing rules for machine-readable data files, computer files, electronic resources, computer programs, interactive multimedia, moving images, or realia. de Groat explains the institutional structure behind this problem: descriptive cataloging rules, Library of Congress practices, subject and genre vocabularies, name/title authority files, and MARC encoding all developed through systems built primarily for books and later adapted to nonbook materials.

The descriptive-cataloging history shows repeated lag between technology and standards. Early AACR2 rules for "machine-readable data files" focused on data and programs rather than interactive consumer software. Early guidance even suggested that cartridge-based video games might be treated as realia, while later ALA guidelines placed them under computer-file rules. Across AACR2 revisions, terminology shifted from "machine-readable data file" to "computer file" to "electronic resource," and physical descriptions shifted from program-file statements to computer cartridges, computer disks, optical discs, CD-ROMs, DVDs, and conventional terminology. These changes made it difficult to collocate like resources or reliably identify particular carriers and platforms.

The article is especially useful on MARC workarounds. MARC created fields for computer files, type of computer file, physical description fixed fields, file characteristics, and system details, but the values and fields were not designed around the complexity of video games. Platform information was often trapped in a system requirements note, a 538 field, or even improvised into an edition statement. The 500 general note became a place where catalogers put useful information that had no better structured field. RDA and later MARC 21 fields added more granular content, media, carrier, sound, video, and digital-file elements, but de Groat argues that these fields still describe simpler electronic resources better than interactive, multi-component games.

The subject and genre discussion is the article's strongest GBLS contribution. de Groat shows that Library of Congress Subject Headings and genre practices were built around books and works about games, not the cataloging of games themselves. Game-genre headings existed as topical headings for books about games rather than as genre/form headings for games. The distinction between "computer games," "video games," "electronic games," "simulation games," "software," and "interactive multimedia" remained unstable. Some headings could be used through workarounds; others could not. Platform or brand headings such as Sega Dreamcast video games were sometimes used even though platform is not a genre. de Groat concludes that the entire field of game genre headings and subdivisions needs overhaul.

Title authority practice creates another layer of difficulty. Library catalogs need titles not only to describe an item in hand but also to collocate versions, translations, adaptations, franchises, and works about games. de Groat explains that computer program titles moved awkwardly between subject and name authority files, while rules for preferred titles under RDA did not give specific guidance for video games. Catalogers had to check authority files and bibliographic files for conflicts, decide whether a game title conflicted with a film, television program, subject heading, or other game, and choose qualifiers such as "Computer game," "Video game," or "Game" without clear rules. This is especially important for games tied to franchises, ports, editions, sequels, and cross-media works.

For the larger GBLS project, de Groat provides historical grounding for the claim that game collections require infrastructure, not merely enthusiasm. If catalogs cannot reliably express platform, carrier, interactivity, genre, subject, franchise, preferred title, version, or relationship to other media, patrons and staff cannot easily discover, compare, recommend, preserve, or study games. This matters for public access, collection development, circulation, curriculum support, game studies scholarship, archives, and future digital preservation. The article pairs well with Welhouse et al. (2015) on narrative/plot metadata and with later artifact-metadata work by Lee et al. and McDonald et al.

The limitations are those of a standards-history article. de Groat does not evaluate patron search behavior, local catalog interfaces, or the effect of catalog records on circulation or use. It is also necessarily technical. Still, it is one of the most important infrastructure sources for GBLS because it shows that the metadata problem is not incidental; it is rooted in decades of rule changes, MARC constraints, Library of Congress policy gaps, and the mismatch between book-centered cataloging traditions and complex interactive media.

A History of Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries
GRETA DE GROAT
Stanford University Libraries, Stanford, California, USA
Cataloging practices for video games have been in ﬂux since the late 1970s, often lagging behind technological developments. This article describes the history of descriptive cataloging practices and MARC coding for video games. Also discussed are the special problems presented by Library of Congress subject heading and uniform title practice, which were developed for book cataloging rather than for the cataloging of video games themselves.
KEYWORDS descriptive cataloging, subject cataloging, video games, Anglo-American Cataloguing Rules (AACR2), MARC 21 formats, Resource Description and Access (RDA), Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH)
As relatively new materials to library collections, video games have been an awkward ﬁt into the regular library cataloging workﬂow. As games developed and non-book materials became more integrated into library cataloging, the cataloging rules and practices have struggled to keep up and to ﬁnd adequate ways of representing these materials. There have never been speciﬁc rules for video games. Descriptive cataloging has usually fallen under the more general rules for computer-related materials which have been in constant ﬂux. Subject/genre practices are based on practice for books, often with unfortunate results. These issues have led to confusion and inconsistency in catalog records.
Descriptive cataloging rules in the United States are governed by an international committee of representatives from the English-speaking cataloging world, formerly the Joint Steering Committee for the Revision of AACR (now the Joint Steering Committee for Development of RDA). The domain

136

G. de Groat

of descriptive cataloging rules is the description of the resource in physical terms, the mode of its usage, and names of creators and other associated persons or corporate bodies. These are reﬁned and extended by the Library of Congress, who publishes their interpretations of the rules and their practices and keeps a list of “authorized” names—the form of names to be used in catalog entries. These practices are followed by most libraries in the United States. The Library of Congress controls the domain of the subject and genre. These are governed by Library of Congress practices and are not based on the descriptive cataloging rules. Finally, all of this data must be encoded in a data structure for dissemination among libraries and use in online catalogs. The standard in the United States is MARC 21, formerly US MARC. This data structure, created in the late 1960s for the purpose of printing catalog cards, divides data into ﬁelds, subﬁelds, and byte strings for machine manipulation. Some of the ﬁelds were designed for indexing, some were not. All of the data called for in the descriptive and subject cataloging rules must be encoded (sometimes shoehorned) into one or more MARC ﬁelds.

DESCRIPTIVE CATALOGING RULES
Neither the ALA Cataloging Rules (1941)1 nor Anglo-American Cataloging Rules, North American Edition (1967)2 mention computer materials. Chapter 12 Revised: Audio Visual Media and Special Instruction Materials, published separately in 1975 after consultations between LC and the Joint Advisory Committee on Nonbook Materials, gave guidelines for many types of audio visual materials, but stated: “Only machine-readable data ﬁles were deleted as a category from the original list of media; the authors feel that this material requires further study before cataloging rules can be properly formulated.”3
The ﬁrst appearance of any guidelines is in Anglo-American Cataloguing Rules, Second Edition (AACR2; 1978), as Chapter 9, machine-readable data ﬁles. These are deﬁned as: “a body of information coded by methods that require the use of a machine (typically a computer) for processing. . . . The term machine readable data ﬁle embraces both the data stored in machine-readable form and the programs used to process that data.”4
This ﬁrst AACR2 Chapter 9 is not lengthy, but does give a few speciﬁc instructions applicable to machine readable data ﬁles.
The “chief source of information (9.0B1),” the place that a cataloger is instructed to use as the source of the title and much other information, is speciﬁed as the “internal user label,” when the resource has one. Rule 9.1C allows the optional addition of a “general material designation” [machinereadable data ﬁle] following the title. Despite its optional status, the general material designation (or GMD) has generally been used by U.S. libraries for nonprint materials. As with other AACR2 rules for edition, 9.2B3, allows catalogers to “supply a suitable brief statement . . . and enclose in square

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

137

brackets” when a ﬁle lacks an edition statement but is known to contain “signiﬁcant changes from previous editions.” Rule 9.2B4 instructs catalogers to not consider minor changes or a change in physical form to be a new edition. This seems rather startling at ﬁrst until one consults the File Description Area (9.5), which is unique to this chapter. The .5 designation in other chapters refers to the extent and physical description, but in chapter 9 it referred to data ﬁles without reference to physical media. Accompanying material (9.5D) may be recorded, whether it is textual (e.g., a codebook) or is another program. This results in statements such as:
1 data ﬁle (3,000 logical records) + 1 program ﬁle 1 program ﬁle (300 statements, COBOL) + 1 data ﬁle (1,000 logical records) 1 data ﬁle (30,000 logical records) + 1 codebook (24 p. ; 25 cm.) + 1
codebook (990 logical records)
Video games already existed by the time that the Chapter 9 rules were ﬁrst published, but I have been unable to ﬁnd any examples cataloged according to this ﬁrst iteration of Chapter 9 prior to 1983.
The ﬁrst manual dedicated to computer materials was Sue A. Dodd’s Cataloging Machine-Readable Data Files: An Interpretive Manual, 1982, which only mentions video games in passing.5 In 1983, Nancy Olson produced A Manual of AACR2 Examples for Microcomputer Software and Video Games, based on the 1978 AACR2 rules. At that time, the American Library Association Committee on Cataloging: Description and Access (CC:DA) had a task force preparing guidelines for cataloging computer software, and Olson’s Manual records the task force recommendation as “the projected guidelines also cover electronic toys or games that are typically issued in cartridge carriers and are manipulated by hand controls. These latter materials are technically under Chapter 10, and a GMD from chapter 10 should probably be used. . .”6 This means they recommended video games be treated as realia. The task force also advocated a compromise with the 1978 rules, adding to the program statement rules in area 9.5 an indication that the ﬁles were on some physical medium,7 with resulting examples such as:
program ﬁles in 1 computer cartridge (Atari 2066)
The examples in the Manual show games cataloged with a mix of practices; having the GMD [game] from Chapter 10 and the program ﬁles statement from Chapter 9—while omitting the actual number of program ﬁles as they were impossible for a cataloger to ascertain.8 However by the time that the ALA published Guidelines for Using AACR2 Chapter 9 for Cataloging Microcomputer Software in 1984, the scope includes the statement: “These guidelines apply to electronic games of the kind typically issued in cartridge carriers and manipulated by hand controls.”9 A System requirements note is explicitly advocated in the American Library Association (ALA) Guidelines, along with a Disk characteristics note.10

138

G. de Groat

Examples show games cataloged purely according to Chapter 9. The ALA Guidelines also codify the addition of the physical medium, but with the programming language and/or computer name attached to the program statement rather than the statement of medium,11 as in:

1 program ﬁle (MBASIC, C-BASIC) on 1 computer disk 1 program ﬁle (PASCAL, Apple IIe) on 1 computer disk

1985’s Cataloging Microcomputer Files: A Manual of Interpretation for AACR2 by Dodd and Ann Sandberg-Fox noted that the software ﬁeld was evolving so quickly that rules were becoming rapidly outdated.12 They also offered an explanation of another recommendation in the ALA Guidelines that had far-reaching consequences in cataloging of computer materials—to record all technical information, such as make and model of machine, memory, operating system, and so on in a single Systems requirement note.13 This was so that the user did not have to look at more than one place within the catalog record for the technical information. This made sense at a time when catalog records were not long out of the card environment and were searchable only by title, author, and subject. In this environment, it was reasonable to optimize the catalog record for human eyes, since the possibility of machine manipulation of the data was very limited and the computer ﬁeld’s rapid evolution made it difﬁcult to standardize vocabulary that would be useful in indexing.
The 1988 revision of AACR2 documents several changes in practice. The most obvious change is that Chapter 9 was now called “Computer ﬁles.” The deﬁnition from 1978 has been replaced by the simpler statement: “The rules in this chapter cover the description of ﬁles that are encoded for manipulation by computer. These ﬁles comprise data and programs. Computer ﬁles may be stored on, or contained in, carriers available for direct access or remote access.”14 Electronic devices themselves or programs residing in their memory would be cataloged according to Chapter 10 as three-dimensional artefacts or realia. The instruction for the chief source of information from 9.0B1 has evolved from taking the title from an “internal user label” to using the “title screen,” and, when lacking, from the physical carrier or labels, and so on. There is a footnote, however, to note that the title screen may not be “available” because of the cataloger’s lack of access to equipment to mount or read the ﬁle. For practical purposes, this means that if a cataloger does not mount the ﬁle, they are to take the title from the label. This has implications for video games, as many catalogers do not routinely run the game and generally do not have the equipment available to do so. With this in mind, in the 1988 revision it was speciﬁed in rule 9.7B3 to always give a source of title note for computer ﬁle materials. In addition, in keeping with the change in the chapter title, the GMD was changed to [computer ﬁle].

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

139

A new data element, File characteristics, was added in the 1988 rules (rule 9.3, speciﬁcally excluded in the 1978 rules). This area incorporates data that in the 1978 rules was put in the “ﬁle description area” in 9.5, which corresponded to the physical description area for other types of materials. The terms computer data, computer program(s) or computer data, and program(s) are speciﬁed as the vocabulary to be used, in addition to parenthetical information on ﬁles, records, and statements, when known.
The rapid development in commercial computer carriers made it necessary for a re-thinking of 9.5 physical description, which is why the ﬁle data was moved to 9.3. In its place in 9.5 are instructions which more accurately parallel the descriptive practices for other materials: number and type of physical units, sound and color, and dimensions. There is a list of general terms for physical units and a list of more speciﬁc terms, which are the ones which libraries have generally used. These are computer chip cartridge, computer tape cartridge, computer tape reel, computer laser optical disk, computer laser optical card. One is generally to give dimensions in inches, except for computer cards, which are given in centimeters, or reels, which are given no dimensions. In the Notes area, rule 9.7B1 mandated the System requirements note, which had been recommended in the ALA Guidelines of 1984. Catalogers are instructed to make the note based on information readily available and to begin the note with the wording “System requirements,” providing, in this order, the make and model of the computer on which the resource is to run, the amount of memory, operating system, software requirements (including programming language), and the kind and characteristics of required or recommended peripherals. Presumably this information would include the video game platform, although this is not explicitly stated. There are no instructions for a Disk characteristics note.
9.7B1 also instructs the cataloger to note the “nature and scope of the ﬁle unless it is apparent from the rest of the description” and gives the example “Game.” However, apparently most catalogers have considered the nature of the resource “apparent” and do not give this note.
The 1988 version of AACR2 was amended in 1993 and 1999, with a wholesale replacement of Chapter 9 in 2001. Computer resources had been continuing to evolve and there had been notable development of the Internet and World Wide Web, which needed accommodation in the rules. However, as of this date, few games on the web have been cataloged, despite the fact that this is an increasingly common mode of distribution.

140

G. de Groat

Another, more temporary development, was the appearance of guidelines for interactive multimedia. This was deﬁned as: “media residing in one or more physical carriers . . . or on computer networks. Interactive multimedia must exhibit both of these characteristics: (1) user-controlled, nonlinear navigation using computer technology and (2) the combination of two or more media (audio, text, graphics, images, animation, and video) that the user manipulates to control the order and/or nature of the presentation.”15 These seem like relatively ordinary characteristics for computer resources, but at the time they caused consternation because of the combination of types of content and sometimes physical carriers. The new guidelines quickly fell into obsolescence as CD-ROMs and DVD-ROMs became mainstream. The term “interactive multimedia” still hangs on in various places in subject/genre cataloging practice and in the MARC 21 format. The GMD [interactive multimedia] was approved for these materials and is still occasionally encountered in catalog records for computer games.
The title of the new Chapter 9 in the AACR2 2001 amendments was changed to Electronic Resources, and the GMD was duly changed to [electronic resource].16 The rule for 9.0B1 for choice of the chief source of information became “the resource itself.” The prescribed vocabulary for 9.3 was changed to “electronic data,” “electronic program(s),” or “electronic data and program(s).” Examples throughout the chapter were updated, though many archaic ones remained. The names of the physical carriers in 9.5B1 were tweaked: computer disk was retained for diskettes, but for CD-ROMs, computer laser optical disk became computer optical disc. Catalogers were also given the option of using “conventional terminology” to represent the carrier; the examples given were:

1 CD-ROM 2 photo CDs 1 DVD.

Many catalogers began exercising this option, as did the Library of Congress, although no instructions were given for when a cataloger was faced with recordable discs, such as CD-R or DVD-R as to whether that should be recorded in this ﬁeld or in a note. Meanwhile, many catalogers continued to use the wording given in 9.5B1.

This history of constantly changing terminology, plus the “conventional terminology” option with no prescribed vocabulary has led to a wide diversity of physical description formulations for computer games, particularly CD-ROM and DVD-ROM types. This makes it very difﬁcult to consistently identify particular classes of discs in an automated manner.

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

141

Another major revision of AACR2 came out in 2002 with a replacement of all the text.17 This was updated in 2003, 2004, and 2005. Examples were again updated to some extent, but the major changes here were the complete elimination of 9.3 and more tweaking of the physical description area in 9.5B, which generally did not apply to game cataloging.
Resource Description and Access, known as RDA, is the successor to AACR2, and is slowly being adopted across the English speaking world and in many other countries as well.18 While it has been reconﬁgured and given a more rigorous intellectual underpinning and most data elements have been split into separate rules, it is still closely allied with AACR2 and in the commonly used implementation still uses many of the same formulations and punctuation (AACR2 uses International Standard for Bibliographic Description [ISBD] punctuation).19
The major differences between RDA and AACR2 are as follows: The GMD is omitted; instead, data elements are given for the content type, media type, and carrier type. These are repeatable for different forms of content contained in the resource. For computer-oriented materials, rule 6.9.1.3 gives only the content types “computer program” and “computer dataset.” Video games are considered to be “two-dimensional moving image” or “three-dimensional moving image,” although some catalogers are also adding “computer program.” The same terminology is used for passive or interactive resources (e.g., a CD-ROM consisting of a video ﬁle and separate computer program would receive the same terms as a video game). There is not a good way to indicate interactivity or relative degree of media integration by these terms alone. Since games are considered moving images, the source of title is supposed to be the title frames, although optionally one may use the label on the physical carrier (2.2.2.3), giving a note on the source of the title.
The rules for physical description are similar to AACR2, except that computer discs and diskettes are now designated as “computer disc” unless one is exercising the alternative at rule 3.4.1.3 to use a “term in common usage,” a practice that presents the same challenges as it did in 2001.
The systems requirements note is no longer required except in cases “beyond what is normal and obvious for the type of carrier or type of ﬁle” (3.20.1.3). There are separate data elements for such information as sound or video characteristics and digital ﬁle characteristics (3.16, 3.18–19). However, the terminology for these ﬁelds is limited and appears to be optimized for simple resources consisting of one or more ﬁles of the same type. Complex resources such as video games are not well described by these rules.
I have concentrated in this section on the physical description of computer games, although AACR2/RDA also covers the formulation of access points. Formulation of access points for titles has proved particularly problematic and will be described in a later section.

142

G. de Groat

DATA FORMAT—MARC

When computer-materials became common enough in library collections for libraries to routinely need to catalog them, it became necessary to create not only cataloging rules, but a data structure that would accommodate those catalog records. The MARC format was already in use for library records, and early records had to be input into existing structures for different types of materials. In 1981, a speciﬁc format for machine-readable data ﬁles was approved, and it went into widespread use in 1984.20 Fondly known as the MRDF format (pronounced “merdiff”), it was unusual in that it included some ﬁelds that were not conﬁned to data described in AACR2. MARC was renamed MARC 21 in 1999.21
The MARC bibliographic formats have different ﬁelds and byte sequences that are conditional on a byte in the Leader. Leader/06 determines the format of the record, and determines the workform that a cataloger will see in OCLC or other cataloging system when they input a record. Leader/06 “m” is the value for “computer ﬁle,” which controls the ﬁelds deﬁned for the MRDF format. Most ﬁelds are the same as in other formats, but the unique ﬁelds include the following:

008/26 Type of computer ﬁle. This has a number of different values, such as numeric data, program, representational, document. Interactive multimedia was added as a value at some point, followed by computer games. Early game cataloging frequently used “u” for unknown or “z” for other in this ﬁeld, other catalogers began using the “i” for interactive multimedia and never switched to “g” for games when it became available.
007 Physical description ﬁxed ﬁeld. This ﬁeld is a byte string, with each byte corresponding to a speciﬁc value. This ﬁeld did not become available for electronic resources until the 1990s. The ﬁrst byte (007/00) is “c” for electronic resource, and the meaning of the rest of the bytes is contingent on that value. Other bytes are type of physical carrier, dimensions, color, and sound. There are eight more bytes, which are seldom used outside of an archival context.
256 Computer ﬁle characteristics. This is the MARC ﬁeld where the AACR2 information for rule 9.3 was recorded (i.e., “computer data,” “computer program(s),” “computer data and program(s)”). It was created in 1987, before that the data was put in the 300 ﬁeld. Although it is still valid in the MARC format, it has fallen out of use since 9.3 was deprecated in 2001.
753 System details access to computer ﬁles. This was designed to be an indexed ﬁeld, and has subﬁelds for make and model of machine, programming language, and operating system. However, there are no input conventions and no controlled vocabulary for this ﬁeld, as it is not in either AACR2 nor RDA. Again computer developments quickly outran the instructions, leaving catalogers uncertain of how much detail to include

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

143

in this ﬁeld. Should one input “PC” or “386/33MHz IBM-PC or compatible”? “Game Boy” or “Nintendo Game Boy”? DOS or Windows? With no clear instructions, many catalogers did not use this ﬁeld, leaving platform information accessible only in the 538 note ﬁeld. To compensate, many catalogers bend the rules for edition and put the game platform in the 250 Edition ﬁeld, on the theory that it designates a change from another version—even when there is not another version. There are also numerous note ﬁelds, numbered 500 to 588, some of which are speciﬁc to computer resources but many of which are seldom used because they relate to data sets. Field 538 is used for the Systems requirements note, and though the cataloging rules called for this note to be placed ﬁrst, in many systems the notes displayed in numerical order, placing it far down in the record. Some catalogers also use this ﬁeld for a “Disc characteristics” note to record that a disc is, for example, a CD-ROM or burnable disc, but this note is not called for in the cataloging rules. The 500 note, the “general” note, became the dumping ground for much useful information. Since the approval of RDA, ﬁelds were hastily added to the MARC 21 format to enable the encoding of, ﬁrst, the content type (336), media type (337), and carrier type (338). Later, ﬁelds were added for sound characteristics (344), video characteristics including video systems NTSC and PAL (346), plus ﬁeld 347 for digital ﬁle characteristics with subﬁelds for ﬁle type, ﬁle encoding format, ﬁle size, resolution, regional encoding, and transmission speed. While laudable to ﬁnally have ﬁelds enabling more granular encoding of technical details, the caveats given in the above discussion of RDA rules 3.16, 3.18–19 apply.

SUBJECT-GENRE
Subject and genre headings have historically been intertwined, so it is best to deal with them together.
American libraries generally use Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH).22 These are topical headings, supplemented by headings from the online Library of Congress Name Authority File (NAF) when the names of people or the titles of works are needed as subjects. Individual catalogers may propose subject headings, but their approval is under the editorial control of the Library of Congress (LC). Formerly LC cataloged games according to AACR2. However, they are now within the moving image archives division (currently called Motion Picture Broadcasting and Recorded Sound Division or MBRS), which does not use AACR2 for cataloging.23 Thus, game cataloging at LC no longer falls under the purview of the rules and practices of the Policy and Standards Division, so there is no constituency within that division with a need to make and revise policies for game cataloging. This

144

G. de Groat

leaves a huge policy gap, as most American libraries follow the LC policies for all materials, even the ones that LC does not catalog.
Assigning subject headings may or may not be straightforward. There are some games for which a topic is apparent, such as games re-creating World War 2 battles or Roman civilization, or games involving sports or sport leagues. Other topics are not so clear. What is The Sims about? People? Is the subject heading “Persons” useful for this material? Is Earth Worm Jim really about “Earthworms”? How about fantasy games in imaginary places? “Imaginary places”? Various ﬁght and ﬁrst person shooter games? “Hand to hand ﬁghting”? “Shooting”? It is not clear how useful these topics are, and in any case, assigning subject headings is a notoriously subjective art, and many catalogers provide minimal subject headings for computer games.
There are two methods of providing genre information in a catalog record in an indexed ﬁeld. The most straightforward is to add a form/genre subdivision to the subject heading. Form/genre subdivisions have been in use for some time, though they were not explicitly given a subdivision tag (subﬁeld “v”) in the MARC format to distinguish them from topical subdivisions until the late 1990s. There is a form subdivision –Computer games which may be added to a subject such as the ones above, leading to possibly helpful heading such as Ardennes, Battle of the, 1944–1945—Computer games or possibly unhelpful ones such as Hedgehogs—Computer games. There is also a form/genre subdivision –Interactive multimedia, which some catalogers use for games. There is a subdivision for –Simulation games but it is explicitly deﬁned in LCSH as topical, and not a form or genre, as is –Computer simulation. The subdivision –Software was authorized in the 1990s, prior to the authorization of –Computer games. A workshop given by the LC in San Francisco in 1997 had –Software as the recommended form subdivision for games in the printed handouts, but attendees were given verbal instructions to use the new subdivision –Computer games, as they had not intended the subdivision –Software to apply to all materials in electronic format, just those which were executable programs.24 However, many catalogers still use the subdivision –Software, often after the names of video game genres. The example Computer adventure games–Software is still given in the Subject Headings Manual, H 2070 “Software and works about software.”25 No explanation is given, making it unclear if it is supposed to be referring to a resource that is a computer adventure game, or to a software program to create a computer adventure game.
LC does have headings for game genres. However, they have all been established as topical headings rather than genre headings. These headings were established for the purpose of providing subject headings for books about particular game genres, and are established on an as-needed basis. They do not reﬂect the actual universe of game genres. In the 1990s before games were moved to MBRS, they sometimes used a topical game genre heading as a genre heading, although they still gave it the MARC tagging

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

145

for a subject. It was not until around 2000 that LC began using the MARC tag 655 for genre headings, and a few years further on until LC began distributing authority records speciﬁcally designated as genre headings. They have distributed headings for ﬁlm and television genres, radio, maps but as yet have no video game genre headings in the genre authority ﬁle. Most libraries use the limited video game genre headings, but coded as topics (MARC ﬁeld 650).
Libraries are free to input genre headings coded as genres (MARC 655), but the LC subject headings list is the only list in widespread use. Even though there are no game headings in the genre list, there is a little-known workaround for libraries. The Frequently Asked Questions About Library of Congress Genre/Form26 says that libraries submitting ofﬁcial BIBCO records for the Program for Cooperative Cataloging may use an LC subject heading as a genre tagged 655 #0 if the heading does not have a scope note saying that it is only “about” or “on” works of a given type. Otherwise, if they decide to use the heading it must be tagged as 650 #7, indicating it is a “local” heading.
According to these instructions, the heading “Computer games” may not be used as a genre because it has a scope note specifying it as a topic, but the following headings lack scope notes so may be coded as genres in the records for individual games:
Video games Computer adventure games Computer baseball games Computer chess Computer ﬂight games Computer war games Computer word games Video keno Video poker Video wrestling games Educational games Simulation games Fantasy games (this is a narrower term from Role playing)
LC also has headings for speciﬁc brands of games, such as Sega Dreamcast video games, but not for all brands. Again, these were established because a book was being cataloged that was about a particular brand of games. Many catalogers also use these as genre or topical headings on the records for individual video games, although it is not clear that this is permissible or useful. Platform and brand information does not seem to be a genre by any stretch of the imagination.
Note that the authorized genre subdivision for a topic is —Computer games. Note that the term “Computer games” is not authorized by itself as a genre, although various types of computer games are able to be used by

146

G. de Groat

the workaround noted above, as is the term Video games. In cataloging a single game, most catalogers are currently using the topic —Computer games construction when possible, and adding one of the more speciﬁc genres, like Computer war games. If the game is played on a computer, they stop there. If it is played on a video game device such as Xbox or Game Boy or PlayStation, they also add the heading Video games.
This begs the question: Is there a difference between computer games and video games? LC seems to think so, since both headings exist in the LCSH list, with individual game titles given as narrower terms, and the lists do not overlap (see the next section on titles for more details about the game titles in the subject ﬁle). What constitutes a video game and what constitutes a computer game is not deﬁned. Catalogers generally suppose that it means that if a game is played on a computer it is a computer game and if it is played on a dedicated video game device it is a video game. However, since the only permissible game subdivision is –Computer games and catalogers often use a mix of “computer” and “video” game headings on the same records, it is not clear at all if this distinction is meaningful or helpful. Should all be “Video games”? There is already a LCSH for “Electronic games,” which is a broader term to both Video games and Computer games. Should that be used instead? It has a scope note that forbids its use as a genre. And there are electronic games that are not video games.
Clearly the entire ﬁeld of game genre headings and subdivisions needs to be completely overhauled.

A SPECIAL NOTE ON TITLES
In library cataloging, the title of the resource may serve different functions in a bibliographic record. The title is, of course, the title of the resource the cataloger is holding in hand. However, if a resource were previously issued under another title or in a different language, there is a need to bring together all versions of the title so that all versions may be found by the catalog user. An additional title is used for this, called a “uniform title” under AACR2 and the “preferred title” under RDA. For materials with one or two creators, this is formulated by using author’s name (last name ﬁrst) followed by a period and the original title of the resource. For materials of diffuse creation, including most modern games, the title alone is used. In addition to the collocation function, this uniform title may also be used as a subject heading for works which are about the work (e.g., a strategy book for a particular video game). The use of the uniform title was considered optional under AACR2 except for libraries following the LC guidelines, particularly libraries contributing Bibliographic Record Cooperative Program (BIBCO) records as part of the Program for Cooperative Cataloging. As the uniform title might be identical to the title of the item being cataloged, there were only certain circumstances

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

147

when one would make an additional entry for this uniform title, and still more restricted circumstances when one would contribute a record to the LC NAF. Therefore, the creation of a name authority record seldom occurred when one was cataloging the game itself.
When LC ﬁrst needed to use the titles of computer resources, they were generally cataloging books about a computer program rather than the program itself. Therefore, their rules called for establishing the titles as subject headings. Titles which were established in the subject ﬁle were speciﬁcally coded as topical subject headings, and could therefore not be used as name headings. When catalogers began cataloging the computer resources themselves, they needed to use name headings as names, not topical subjects. Since 1987, the LC documentation instructs their catalogers to establish the headings for “individual computer programs . . . when needed, in the name authority ﬁle.”27 When cataloging a work about a program, catalogers are to search the LC database to see whether a name authority record or bibliographic record has been created already and use the form of title found there, but code it in their bibliographic record as a title subject (MARC 630) rather than a topical subject. If the program has not been cataloged by LC, they are to have someone in the Special Materials Cataloging Division establish a heading in the NAF. If a conﬂict is found with something else with the same title, the qualiﬁer (Computer ﬁle) is added to the title. No instructions are given for the case where the heading is already established as a topical subject (MARC 650), therefore not useable as a title subject.
When cataloging a computer resource itself, no speciﬁc instructions were given for establishing a “uniform title” aside from the general rules for all materials. As the title would only be needed in the rare instances when it was previously issued under another title or in a different language, the issue rarely came up (and when it did, it was sometimes because a game had been issued previously in Japan and likely the cataloger could not determine the authorized Japanese form in order to use the original title). In 1987, when the LC was to cease establishing the titles of computer programs as subjects, they added a rule interpretation to 25.5B stating that if a computer program were entered under the same title as the title work, do not assign a uniform title simply to distinguish, but if the uniform title were otherwise needed, use the qualiﬁer (Computer program), and then use that uniform title, with the qualiﬁer, for all entries for that particular work—including changing other bibliographic records for that work.28 Like similar rules for motion pictures and television programs, this was widely ignored by the cataloging community, and apparently by LC as well since it is contradicted by the aforementioned policy in the Subject Cataloging Manual.29 Some minor clariﬁcations were made over the years, but the rule interpretation was deleted with Cataloging Service Bulletin no. 99 (Winter 2003).

148

G. de Groat

Note that in both the Subject cataloging rules and in the LC rule interpretations, the subject under discussion was “computer programs.” Computer or video games are not mentioned in the discussion of title practice. Does this mean that these rules did not cover games? Most catalogers assumed that the rules were intended to cover all computer materials, a feeling reinforced when in the late 1990s the rule interpretation for computer programs was expanded to cover electronic serials, which were clearly not “programs.”
With the adoption of RDA, the LC in its Policy Statement for 6.27.1.9 of June 2012 declared that for monographically issued resources the cataloger must add a preferred title with a qualiﬁer in case of a conﬂict.30 It gives general rules for all types of materials but no speciﬁc clues for computer resources or games. It offers up the choice of corporate body, date of publication, “descriptive data elements” (e.g., edition statement), place of publication, or if none of those are appropriate, “any word(s) that will serve to distinguish the one work from the other,” and prescribes the qualiﬁers (Motion picture), (Television program), (Comic strip), (Choreographic work), and (Radio program) for those types of works. With the slow adoption of RDA, many catalogers have not yet seen nor realized the implications of this policy change, and in any case they are binding only on the LC, which no longer catalogs computer games in their library workﬂow, and libraries contributing BIBCO records to the Program for Cooperative Cataloging.
To summarize the labyrinthine situation, catalogers for video games who are following the Library of Congress policies for preferred title must check at least the LC NAF and bibliographic ﬁle, as well as any other cooperative catalog (such as OCLC) with which the cataloger works, to determine whether the title of the game being cataloged is the same as that of another work which is also entered under title. If a work with the same title is found, it is considered a conﬂict. If a game is a franchise related to a television program or motion picture, it will almost surely be a conﬂict. It will also conﬂict if a cataloger chooses to give the franchise title as the title and the distinctive game titles as a subtitle (a common, but problematic practice). The title also must not conﬂict with an LC subject heading, a not-infrequent occurrence with one-word titles. If a conﬂict is found, the cataloger must add a qualiﬁer to the title, but no instructions are given for video games. Should the cataloger follow the rules for monographs (publisher, date, etc.) or follow the pattern for movies and television programs? The latter is more user-friendly but in the absence of a speciﬁc directive, one assumes that the general rule for monographs should be followed. However, looking back at the Subject Cataloging manual rule for H2070, which has not been superseded so is still in force, it still says to use the qualiﬁer (Computer ﬁle) when the title is needed as a title subject, even though it is to be established in the name ﬁle. This is assuming that games are supposed to be covered under that rule, which is not entirely clear. Catalogers (in the rare instances that they are establishing these titles in the name authority ﬁle) clearly do not

Video Game Cataloging in U.S. Libraries

149

know what qualiﬁer to use, as a search through the name authority ﬁle will show headings using (Computer game), (Video game), (Game), and so on. To further muddy the waters, even though names of “computer programs” are supposed to have been established in the NAF since 1987, new game titles are continuing to be added to the subject ﬁle, for example, “Angry Birds (Game),” added in April 2012 and “Angry Birds: Star Wars (Game),” added in October 2012. The qualiﬁer (Game) is being given to these titles in the subject ﬁle, the same as is used for a traditional or board game, though there are no explicit rules in the subject cataloging manuals for games of any type. There are no instructions for what to do when the title would conﬂict with the name of another type of game such as Monopoly (Game) or Solitaire (Game)—though there is also a subject heading for Solitaire (Board game).

CONCLUSION
Video game cataloging has undergone a great evolution since the general appearance of catalogable computer resources in the late 1970s. Cataloging rules and the MARC 21 have been frequently updated as the characteristics of the materials changed, usually lagging behind by a few years. Consistent terminology has been elusive, making it difﬁcult to collocate materials or provide a consistent search or limit strategy to ﬁnd all like materials. RDA and the recent MARC 21 updates have provided more granularity and some controlled vocabulary, but continue to better describe simple electronic resources and data ﬁles than complex media like games and other interactive works.
In contrast to the somewhat improving situation regarding the descriptive cataloging rules and MARC, the policies for subjects, genres, and uniform/preferred titles remain intractable. The LC sets the policies that most American libraries follow for uniform/preferred titles of works and for subject and genres headings. But as they no longer catalog games within the library section of their operations, their policies are inadequate and contradictory for these materials. They have had neither the mandate nor the stafﬁng to undertake the task of completely reevaluating their policies on game genres headings, subject headings, and name heading policies to bring some consistency and logic to them.
In the meantime, catalogers continue reading the tea leaves to try to ﬁgure out how they are do deal with these complex, yet ubiquitous materials.
